does: This paper introduces K-BrowseComp, a benchmark of 400 web-browsing agent problems grounded in Korean language and cultural contexts, designed to evaluate frontier language models on compositional, agentic capabilities. The benchmark comprises 300 manually validated problems created by native Korean speakers and 100 adversarially generated synthetic problems designed to expose failure modes. The authors evaluate state-of-the-art models including GPT-5.5, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and GLM-5.1, finding they achieve only 30–45.67% on the verified subset compared to higher performance on the English BrowseComp benchmark, while Korean-specific LLMs perform substantially worse at 0–10.33%. The synthetic diagnostic split, filtered for adversarial difficulty, reveals a further performance drop to 26% for the strongest model, demonstrating the benchmark's effectiveness as a targeted stress test for web navigation and task completion.
connects: This work builds on the established BrowseComp benchmark and broader shifts in LLM evaluation from foundational capabilities like instruction following to agentic, task-oriented benchmarks requiring interactive planning and execution. It responds to a documented gap: while English-language web-browsing agent benchmarks have become standard for evaluating frontier models, comparable benchmarks for non-English languages and cultural contexts remain sparse. The paper extends methodologies from recent synthetic benchmark generation work, leveraging hard few-shot exemplars and failure-mode-targeted generation to create challenging evaluation scenarios. It also engages with the growing recognition that multilingual and culturally grounded benchmarks are essential for understanding model capabilities and limitations across diverse real-world deployment contexts.
enables: This benchmark enables more rigorous evaluation of language models intended for Korean markets and multilingual applications, exposing capability gaps that generic English benchmarks might obscure. The stark performance differential between frontier English models and Korean-specific models provides concrete motivation for improving Korean language understanding and agentic reasoning. The adversarial synthetic diagnostic split offers a template for creating targeted stress tests that expose specific failure modes, which future work can apply to other languages and domains. By releasing data and code, the benchmark supports iterative improvement of Korean LLMs and establishes evaluation standards for the field. The work also points toward developing culturally aware agent benchmarks for other languages and demonstrates that web browsing—a practical, real-world task—serves as an effective testbed for measuring compositional reasoning in models adapted to non-English contexts.
